frameworks.name: drift-diffusion model
frameworks.one_line: characterizes evidence accumulation dynamics underlying trans-saccadic perceptual choices

frameworks.name: Bayesian observer model
frameworks.one_line: captures choices but is insensitive to reaction times

frameworks.name: trans-saccadic memory
frameworks.one_line: pre-saccadic stimulus held and compared with post-saccadic input

core_question: How is perceptual decision-making implemented when evidence must be integrated across saccadic eye movements?
key_assumptions: trans-saccadic perception relies on a memory of the pre-saccadic stimulus compared to post-saccadic input
sensory evidence accumulates continuously across discrete saccadic views
perceptual choices can be modeled as either Bayesian inference or bounded evidence accumulation
manipulations: fixation strategy: maintain fixation vs. transient re-fixation on the first target
orientation difference between sequentially presented Gabor patches
spatial location of the two Gabor targets in a fast sequence
key_findings: Bayesian observer fit choices but not reaction times
drift-diffusion model captured both choices and choice times
trans-saccadic perception did not slow decisions or reduce accuracy
evidence accumulation rate was lower under trans-saccadic perception and correlated trial-by-trial with accuracy and choice time
interpretations: perception across saccades reflects an active, dynamic integration of sensory evidence rather than passive memory comparison
drift-diffusion provides a more complete account of trans-saccadic decisions than a static Bayesian observer
limitations_noted: Bayesian observer model is insensitive to choice reaction times
